result:
# Static

The radio in my grandmother's kitchen never picked up anything clean. Every station came through with a kind of gauze over it, a soft interference that made every song sound like it was being played in a room one door over. She never fixed it. I used to think she couldn't afford to, or didn't know how, but now I think she preferred it that way. The world kept at a slight distance. Music you had to lean toward.

She died in February, which seems like the right month for it. Not because February is especially morbid but because it is the month with the least conviction. It doesn't fully commit to being winter. It doesn't have the courage to be spring. It just exists, grey and uncertain, waiting to be relieved of duty.

I drove back to her house alone because everyone else had things going on and I said that was fine, I don't mind, I'll handle it. This is something I say often. I'll handle it. As if I am a surface that things can be placed on without worry.

---

The house smelled the way all old houses smell when they've been shut up for a while. Not bad exactly. More like time made physical. Like if you could press your face against the neck of a decade and breathe in.

I stood in the kitchen for a long time before I touched anything.

The radio was still on the counter. I don't know why this surprised me. Where would it have gone? I plugged it in mostly to prove that it wouldn't work, that something would have changed, that the world at least follows some rules of entropy and loss. But it turned on. The same static. The same gauze. Somewhere inside all that warm noise, a song I didn't recognize.

I sat down on the floor, which is not something I do often. Being on the floor feels like an admission. I sat there and I listened and I thought about all the things I never said to her that I now carry like stones in the pockets of every coat I own.

---

There is a theory I have, completely unprovable, that the people we love don't go anywhere. They just become ambient. They become the thing underneath everything. The frequency that other frequencies rest on. You can't isolate it. You can't tune to it directly. But when it goes, you notice the silence in the music, the missing floor, the way everything sounds slightly thinner than it did before.

This is probably grief talking. Grief is a very creative writer. It will construct elaborate metaphysics to avoid saying the simple thing, which is: I miss her, and missing her feels like trying to remember a word you knew perfectly well yesterday.

---

I grew up being told I was too sensitive, which I internalized as a criticism for decades before I understood it was just information. Yes. I am sensitive. I feel the static. I hear the song in the interference. Not everyone does. This is not a virtue or a flaw. It is simply what I am, and it has cost me things and given me things in roughly equal measure, though the ledger never balances cleanly.

She was sensitive too, my grandmother. She just called it something else. She called it paying attention.

*Pay attention*, she used to say, as if I was about to walk past something important. As if the world was constantly offering something and most people just kept moving.

I think of all the things I walked past anyway. All the things I was too busy or too scared or too small to receive. I think of all the times I mistook paying attention for something weaker than not paying attention, when in fact it is the harder work, the more dangerous work, the work that leaves you sitting on the floor of an empty kitchen listening to a song you can't quite hear.

---

Before I left I took the radio. I didn't take much else. My cousins would come for the furniture and the dishes and the photographs. I let them have all of it. I took the radio.

It sits on my counter now. I don't always plug it in. But sometimes at night when the apartment is too quiet and the quiet has a quality I can't name, I plug it in and I turn it on and I stand in my kitchen in the dark with the static and whatever is hiding inside the static.

I don't know if it helps. I don't know if anything helps, exactly. I think you just get to a point where helping and not helping stop being the right categories. Where you stop asking whether it's working and start asking whether it's honest. Whether you're showing up to the thing. Whether you're paying attention.

She would have said that was enough. She would have said that was the whole job.

I'm choosing to believe her. I don't have the radio for very many other reasons. I have it because I want one more way to practice leaning toward something I can't quite make out. One more way to sit in the gauze and the warmth and the noise and say: I know something's there. I can almost hear it.

I'll keep listening.

That seems like something she would have wanted. That seems, in February and out of it, like the right thing to do.
